title: Whether a dying craft deserves a subsidy when no one wants to learn it
summary: Argue whether public money should keep a fading trade alive when the next generation has no interest in practising it.
topic: Whether dying crafts should be publicly funded when the next generation is uninterested
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether public funding should keep a vanishing craft alive — Neapolitan puppet-making, traditional Tashkent ceramics, hand-tooled bookbinding in Edinburgh — when fewer and fewer young people want to spend ten years learning it. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one named craft, city, or workshop. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What public subsidy for a dying craft is actually paying for is rarely the craft itself, but…
Granted, the romantic case for preserving every traditional trade can shade into a kind of cultural taxidermy that helps neither…
In a workshop near the harbour in Naples, the last full-time maker of marionettes for the local puppet theatre is now…

hintVocab.meaningVi: trợ cấp các kỹ năng di sản
di sản văn hóa phi vật thể
biến một truyền thống thành hàng hóa

hintVocab.phrase: to be priced out by industrial production
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị loại khỏi thị trường bởi sản xuất công nghiệp
teo dần do không được sử dụng

hintVocab.phrase: to be reduced to museum display
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị thu nhỏ thành trưng bày bảo tàng
việc nhồi bông di sản (giữ xác mà không còn sự sống)
tìm thấy một ứng dụng đương đại
biến một nghề thành sản phẩm cao cấp

hintVocab.phrase: to bear no resemblance to the original
hintVocab.meaningVi: không còn giống bản gốc chút nào

sampleText:
In a workshop near the harbour in Naples, the last full-time maker of marionettes for the local puppet theatre is now in his late seventies. He has trained no successor. The municipality has, for several years, offered a modest stipend to anyone who will study under him; only one young woman has accepted, and she splits her time with a graphic design job to make rent. When he stops, what he knows stops with him.

Whether public money should be used to prevent that ending is a more complicated question than either side usually allows. The romantic case for preservation tends to assume that every disappearing craft deserves rescue. The opposing case tends to assume that if nobody wants to do the work, the work was no longer needed. Both positions skip past the question that actually matters.

What public subsidy for a dying craft is actually paying for is rarely the craft itself, in any narrow sense. The world will not be measurably poorer for the absence of one more marionette workshop. What is being funded, when funding works, is the conditions under which a particular kind of knowledge can be transmitted: time, tools, a roof, a master willing to teach, and a learner willing to be slow. Without those conditions, even a craft with genuine contemporary value disappears in a generation.

Admittedly, not every fading trade deserves this treatment. Some crafts vanish because the thing they made is no longer needed, no longer wanted, and no longer interesting except as a curiosity. A subsidy for a dying craft whose products no one will ever use again is not heritage protection; it is a form of cultural taxidermy. Public money is finite, and choices have to be made about which knowledge is worth keeping live.

Even so, the test cannot simply be whether young people are currently lining up to learn. Apprenticeship was never an instinct in any era; it was a structure. Crafts survived for centuries because guild systems, apprentice stipends, and steady commissions made it economically possible to spend ten years becoming competent. Where modern markets no longer provide that scaffolding for crafts that still have living usefulness — restoration, conservation, specialist repair — public subsidy is doing exactly what public goods are for.

Granted, the line between living craft and museum exhibit is not always obvious. A reasonable policy would fund crafts that retain genuine contemporary application, with clear succession plans and meaningful demand; it would not preserve a workshop simply because someone once filmed it for a documentary. The question worth asking before any subsidy is signed off is not whether the craft is old, but whether anyone, in twenty years, will still need what it makes.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Whether public money should be used to prevent that ending is a more complicated question than either side usually allows

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Both positions skip past the question that actually matters

sampleAnnotations.phrase: A subsidy for a dying craft whose products no one will ever use again is not heritage protection; it is a form of cultural taxidermy

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Nơi mà các thị trường hiện đại không còn cung cấp giàn giáo đó cho các nghề vẫn có tính hữu ích sống động

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the line between living craft and museum exhibit is not always obvious
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: ranh giới giữa nghề thủ công đang sống và hiện vật bảo tàng không phải lúc nào cũng rõ ràng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: A reasonable policy would fund crafts that retain genuine contemporary application

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The question worth asking before any subsidy is signed off is not whether the craft is old, but whether anyone, in twenty years, will still need what it makes

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to reframe what is being paid for
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What public subsidy for a dying craft is actually paying for is rarely the craft itself, in any narrow sense' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để buộc người đọc xem xét lại đối tượng được tài trợ. Cấu trúc này cho phép tác giả tách bạch hành động chi trả với mục tiêu thực sự, và cụm phủ định 'rarely the craft itself' nằm ở vị trí được nhấn mạnh cuối câu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What public subsidy for a dying craft is actually paying for is rarely the craft itself, in any narrow sense' uses a what-cleft to make the reader re-examine what is actually being funded. The structure separates the act of paying from the real target, and the negative clause 'rarely the craft itself' lands in the emphatic position at the end.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Pseudo-cleft with continuous passive for definitional precision
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What is being funded, when funding works, is the conditions under which a particular kind of knowledge can be transmitted' kết hợp cấu trúc pseudo-cleft ('What is being funded… is…') với dạng bị động tiếp diễn ('is being funded'). Cụm chèn 'when funding works' đặt điều kiện cho định nghĩa, và cấu trúc tổng thể cho phép tác giả định nghĩa lại một thuật ngữ chính sách trong một câu duy nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What is being funded, when funding works, is the conditions under which a particular kind of knowledge can be transmitted' combines a pseudo-cleft ('What is being funded… is…') with a continuous passive ('is being funded'). The parenthetical 'when funding works' qualifies the definition, and the overall structure lets the writer redefine a policy term in a single sentence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong một xưởng gần bến cảng ở Naples, người làm marionette toàn thời gian cuối cùng cho nhà hát múa rối địa phương hiện đã ngoài bảy mươi. Ông chưa đào tạo người kế nhiệm nào. Chính quyền địa phương, trong vài năm qua, đã đề nghị một khoản trợ cấp khiêm tốn cho bất kỳ ai sẽ học dưới quyền ông; chỉ có một người phụ nữ trẻ chấp nhận, và cô chia thời gian với một công việc thiết kế đồ họa để trả tiền thuê nhà. Khi ông ngừng làm, những gì ông biết cũng ngừng theo.

Liệu tiền công có nên được sử dụng để ngăn chặn cái kết thúc đó hay không là một câu hỏi phức tạp hơn so với những gì mỗi bên thường thừa nhận. Quan điểm lãng mạn về việc bảo tồn có xu hướng giả định rằng mỗi nghề đang biến mất đều xứng đáng được cứu. Quan điểm đối lập có xu hướng giả định rằng nếu không ai muốn làm công việc đó, thì công việc đó không còn cần thiết nữa. Cả hai quan điểm đều bỏ qua câu hỏi thực sự quan trọng.

Điều mà trợ cấp công cho một nghề đang chết thực sự chi trả hiếm khi là chính nghề đó, theo bất kỳ nghĩa hẹp nào. Thế giới sẽ không nghèo hơn một cách đo lường được nếu thiếu thêm một xưởng marionette. Điều đang được tài trợ, khi tài trợ có hiệu quả, là các điều kiện mà một loại kiến thức cụ thể có thể được truyền đạt: thời gian, dụng cụ, một mái nhà, một bậc thầy sẵn lòng dạy, và một người học sẵn lòng đi chậm. Không có những điều kiện đó, ngay cả một nghề có giá trị đương đại thực sự cũng biến mất trong một thế hệ.

Thừa nhận rằng, không phải mọi nghề đang phai mờ đều xứng đáng được đối xử như vậy. Một số nghề biến mất vì những gì chúng tạo ra không còn cần thiết, không còn được mong muốn, và không còn thú vị ngoại trừ như một thứ tò mò. Một khoản trợ cấp cho một nghề đang chết mà sản phẩm của nó không ai sẽ dùng lại không phải là bảo vệ di sản; đó là một hình thức nhồi bông văn hóa. Tiền công là hữu hạn, và phải có những lựa chọn về kiến thức nào đáng được giữ sống.

Dẫu vậy, bài kiểm tra không thể đơn giản là liệu người trẻ có đang xếp hàng để học hay không. Học nghề chưa bao giờ là một bản năng ở bất kỳ thời đại nào; nó là một cấu trúc. Các nghề thủ công tồn tại trong nhiều thế kỷ vì các hệ thống phường hội, trợ cấp học nghề, và các đơn đặt hàng ổn định đã làm cho điều đó khả thi về mặt kinh tế để dành mười năm trở nên thành thạo. Nơi mà các thị trường hiện đại không còn cung cấp giàn giáo đó cho các nghề vẫn có tính hữu ích sống động — phục chế, bảo tồn, sửa chữa chuyên biệt — trợ cấp công đang làm chính xác những gì các hàng hóa công nên làm.

Thừa nhận rằng, ranh giới giữa nghề thủ công đang sống và hiện vật bảo tàng không phải lúc nào cũng rõ ràng. Một chính sách hợp lý sẽ tài trợ các nghề vẫn giữ được ứng dụng đương đại thực sự, với các kế hoạch kế nhiệm rõ ràng và nhu cầu có ý nghĩa; nó sẽ không bảo tồn một xưởng chỉ vì có ai đó đã từng quay phim nó cho một bộ phim tài liệu. Câu hỏi đáng đặt ra trước khi bất kỳ khoản trợ cấp nào được phê duyệt không phải là nghề đó có cũ hay không, mà là liệu có ai, trong hai mươi năm nữa, sẽ vẫn cần những gì nó tạo ra hay không.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to reframe what is being funded?
options: Rarely the craft itself paying for what public subsidy actually is a dying craft.
What public subsidy for a dying craft is actually paying for is rarely the craft itself, in any narrow sense.
A dying craft public subsidy paying actually what is for is rarely itself the craft.
Itself the craft rarely paying for what is actually public subsidy a dying craft.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' places the surprising claim ('rarely the craft itself') in the emphatic position after 'is'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the writer's surprising claim placed after 'is'.

options: The preservation of dead animals for cultural exhibitions
Keeping the outward appearance of a tradition alive after its living function has died
A medical procedure performed on important cultural figures
A type of insurance for valuable cultural objects
mcQuestions.explanation: Taxidermy preserves the outer form of a dead animal; 'cultural taxidermy' is the metaphor for preserving the visible shell of a craft after the actual reason for practising it has disappeared.
mcQuestions.hint: What does taxidermy do to an animal? Apply that to a cultural tradition.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'admittedly' correctly as a concession?
options: Admittedly, not every fading trade deserves this treatment.
Not every admittedly fading trade this treatment deserves.
Deserves this treatment fading admittedly not every trade.
Trade not every admittedly fading deserves this treatment.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' followed by a complete clause grants a real point to the opposing view before the essay continues.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Admittedly,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a continuous passive to redefine what funding actually does?
options: What is being funded, when funding works, is the conditions under which a particular kind of knowledge can be transmitted.
Funding works when what is funded conditions knowledge particular kind transmitted being.
Conditions transmitted being funded what is funding works knowledge particular.
When funding works particular knowledge transmitted being is what funded conditions.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is being funded' is the continuous passive, embedded inside a pseudo-cleft, redefining the subsidy as a process targeting conditions rather than products.
mcQuestions.hint: Continuous passive looks like 'is/are being + past participle'.

options: Knowledge that is written down in a textbook
Knowledge that is hard to express in words and is learned mainly by doing alongside an experienced practitioner
Knowledge that is officially recognised by a guild
Knowledge that is kept secret from the general public
mcQuestions.explanation: Tacit knowledge is the kind of skill — feel, judgement, timing — that cannot be fully captured in words and is transmitted only through hands-on training.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of how an experienced cook 'just knows' when something is done. What kind of knowledge is that?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'Apprenticeship was never an instinct in any era; it was a structure'?
options: Apprenticeship has always been a natural human instinct.
Young people in the past simply chose to learn crafts because they wanted to.
Apprenticeship existed in the past not because people were drawn to it spontaneously but because economic systems made it possible.
The structure of apprenticeship has changed but the instinct remains.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's claim is that apprenticeship never simply happened because of natural enthusiasm; it required guilds, stipends, and steady commissions to make a decade of training economically feasible.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the essay contrasting — 'instinct' against what kind of opposite force?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to find a contemporary application' mean for a craft?
options: To submit a job application using modern online forms
To find a present-day purpose or use that justifies the craft's continued existence
To apply for membership in a modern guild
To file a contemporary copyright on a traditional design
mcQuestions.explanation: A craft 'finds a contemporary application' when it discovers a present-day purpose — restoration, repair, specialist work — that gives its skills a continuing reason to exist.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'application' mean when talking about how a skill is used?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause to describe the master?
options: A master willing to teach, and a learner willing to be slow.
Willing teach to a master and a learner be slow willing to.
Slow to be willing a learner teach to and a master willing.
Learner willing a master be slow to teach to willing a and.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'willing to teach' and 'willing to be slow' are reduced clauses describing 'a master' and 'a learner' respectively — they function like compressed relative clauses.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for adjectival phrases ('willing to + verb') that follow each noun directly.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the test cannot simply be whether young people are currently lining up to learn.
On the contrary, all crafts must die.
Furthermore, no craft has any value.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the concession (not every craft deserves rescue) and pivots back to the main argument that demand alone is not the right test.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All dying crafts deserve public funding regardless of cost.
No dying craft should receive public funding.
Public funding is justified for crafts with genuine contemporary usefulness and a viable succession plan — but not for crafts preserved only as cultural display.
Public funding for crafts should be left entirely to private donors and museums.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay carves out a middle position: fund crafts that retain real present-day use with succession plans, do not fund mere museum displays.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph for the writer's clearest statement of position.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly some crafts are just dead and we should let them go, but others are kind of useful.
Old crafts are basically cool and we should fund them whenever we can.
Public subsidy for traditional skills is justified where genuine contemporary demand exists and where viable transmission pathways can be maintained.
It's pretty obvious that we should pay for the useful crafts and let the others die.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('genuine contemporary demand', 'viable transmission pathways') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal intensifiers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'guild structure' mean in the historical sense the essay uses?
options: A modern trade union for craft workers
A medieval and early-modern organisation of craftspeople that regulated training, standards, and commissions
A government department overseeing traditional industries
A building where craftspeople sell their products to the public
mcQuestions.explanation: Guilds were the historical institutions that made long apprenticeships economically viable by controlling training, quality, prices, and commissions across the craft.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of medieval craftsmen — what institution organised their training and commissions?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: A subsidy for a dying craft whose products no one will ever use again is not heritage protection.
Whose products no one will ever use again a subsidy for a dying craft is not heritage protection.
Heritage protection a subsidy is not whose products no one will ever use a dying craft for again.
A dying craft is not heritage protection whose products a subsidy no one will ever use again for.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'whose products no one will ever use again' is a defining relative clause specifying which dying craft the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'whose', 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to atrophy through disuse' mean?
options: To grow stronger when used frequently
To weaken or waste away when not used over time
To be officially abolished by government
To be misused for the wrong purpose
mcQuestions.explanation: Atrophy is the wasting of something — a muscle, a skill, an institution — that is not being exercised; the verb captures slow, structural decline through neglect rather than sudden loss.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of what happens to a muscle that is never used.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay by posing the right policy question?
options: In conclusion, all old crafts have some kind of value.
Therefore, all subsidies should be abolished immediately.
The question worth asking before any subsidy is signed off is not whether the craft is old, but whether anyone, in twenty years, will still need what it makes.
To sum up, dying crafts are a complicated subject with many sides.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C ends with a sharp, specific policy question that captures the essay's central test — not age but continuing need.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a precise, decision-shaping question rather than a vague summary?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say is the right test for whether a dying craft should be subsidised?
options: Whether young people are currently enthusiastic to learn it
Whether the craft has been featured in a documentary film
Whether the craft has genuine contemporary application and whether anyone will still need what it makes in twenty years
Whether the craft is older than two hundred years
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly rejects enthusiasm-as-test (paragraph five) and museum-status-as-test (paragraph six), proposing instead a continuing-usefulness test.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph for the writer's stated test.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a traditional craft, trade, or practice from your own country that is fading. If a council asked you whether public money should be spent to keep it alive, what specific evidence would you look for before saying yes — and what would push you toward saying no?
